title: 🎙️ Working Lands & Industry
description: Cariboo forestry, ranching, mining, agriculture — tech in the resource economy - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Cariboo forestry, ranching, mining, agriculture — tech in the resource economy
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Working Lands & Industry' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: precision forestry and remote sensing in Cariboo logging, AgTech and ranch management systems, mining automation and safety technology, farm sensors and soil monitoring, sawmill modernization and automation, drone surveys for resource management, IoT in agriculture and ranching, climate adaptation tech for working lands, workforce training for industrial tech, commodity tracking and supply chain tech, and how Cariboo resource industries adopt innovation. Prefer articles that bridge resource economy AND technology in rural/industrial contexts. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.content_html:
Photography by Jake Price “I grew up fishing these waters down here just like everyone down in Shell Beach,” says Brad Robin, whose family has lived here for generations. “We never had a bike ride in the streets. We had a pirogue battle in the canals,” he says, referring to the lightweight canoes used to […]

items.title: [New Atlas] Bio-based, recyclable resins outperform their fossil-fuel counterparts
items.content_html:
To wood or not to wood, that's been the question! Ever since oil-based plastics burst onto the manufacturing scene, cost, sustainability, and performance have defined the ongoing woods vs. plastics war. In many applications, plastics tend to dominate in cost efficiency and durability, while wood retains a clear edge in sustainability.

Continue Reading

items.title: [Yellowhead Institute] The Prison as Reconciliation? Considering the “Indigenization” of Carceral Spaces in Canada
items.content_html:
In this Brief, Ashley Kyne and Justin Piché challenge the “Indigenization” of prisons – often framed as collaborative initiatives with Indigenous communities – arguing that these efforts reproduce colonial space through carceral policies to create ‘culturally meaningful spaces’ that misrepresent Indigenous histories, worldviews, and lived realities, normalizing confinement .

The post The Prison as Reconciliation? Considering the “Indigenization” of Carceral Spaces in Canada appeared first on Yellowhead Institute.

items.title: [NPR Health News] Medical schools agree to expand their nutrition curriculum this fall
